title: Ask the God
subtitle: Oracle Consultation and the Everyday Supernatural Infrastructure of the Ancient World
summary: In 21 CE, a man named Neoteros writes a petition to a local crocodile god: should he travel to Alexandria to bring a legal complaint? Five centuries later, someone asks the Christian God whether Theodora should marry Joseph. Between them, soldiers in the Eastern Desert pull numbered oracle tickets. The supernatural was not sequestered in temples -- it was infrastructure, woven into decision-making, correspondence, child-rearing, and business.

sections.title: I. The God at Karanis
sections.body_html:
Soxis Pnepheros was not Apollo. He was not one of the famous oracles of the Greek world – not Delphi with its Pythian priestess perched above a fissure in the earth, not Dodona with its rustling oak, not Didyma with its sacred spring. Soxis Pnepheros was a local deity of the Fayum, the great oasis-basin southwest of the Nile Delta where an artificial lake, Lake Moeris, sustained a constellation of villages and small towns across reclaimed agricultural land.4

The name reveals his Egyptian roots. “Pnepheros” derives from the Egyptian Pa-nfr-Hr, “The Beautiful One of Horus” – an epithet applied to various gods in the Fayum, often in connection with the crocodile cult.5 Karanis, the village where Neoteros likely submitted his question, was home to two major temples dedicated to crocodile-related gods: the temple of Pnepheros and Petesouchos, built or expanded under Nero, which would later be excavated by the University of Michigan in the 1920s and 1930s.6 Soxis – or Soksis – appears as another local manifestation, part of the dense web of crocodile deities that populated the Fayum landscape.

These were not grand institutions. The temples at Karanis were modest structures of mud brick and limestone, staffed by local priests who also served as farmers, scribes, and tax officials.7 Their gods were intimate, accessible, embedded in the rhythms of agricultural life. You did not need to undertake a pilgrimage to consult them. You walked across the village.

And the questions people brought were not questions of state or war or cosmic destiny. They were the questions of ordinary life.

sections.title: II. The Mechanics of Asking
sections.body_html:
How did the oracle actually work? The surviving evidence from Egypt, accumulated across centuries of papyrological discovery, reveals a remarkably standardised procedure.

The petitioner would write his question on a small piece of papyrus – or have it written, if he was illiterate. In many cases, two tickets were prepared: one with the question phrased positively, one negatively. “Is it granted to me to marry Tapetheus?” on one slip. “Is it not granted to me to marry Tapetheus?” on the other.8 Both were submitted to the priest, who placed them before the god.

The god then “chose” one of the tickets. How this selection occurred is debated. Some temples used processional statues that could be manipulated to nod or incline in a particular direction – a practice attested in pharaonic Egypt and described, with varying degrees of credulity, by classical authors.9 Others may have used a simpler mechanism: the priest entering the sanctuary, emerging with one ticket and not the other. The selected ticket was returned to the petitioner, who kept it as both answer and amulet – divine confirmation in portable form.10

The largest caches of these oracle tickets have come from Fayum temples of the crocodile god Sobek, written in both Demotic Egyptian and Greek, spanning the Ptolemaic and Roman periods.11 Almost half of all surviving Greek oracle questions from Egypt come from a single site: Soknopaiou Nesos – “Island of Soknopaios” – on the northern shore of Lake Moeris, where the crocodile god Soknopaios served as oracle and healing deity.12

One of the most vivid surviving questions comes from this very site. On 26 April in the year 6 CE – fifteen years before Neoteros wrote his petition – a man named Asklepiades son of Areios addressed himself to “the very great and mighty god Soknopaios”:

If it is not granted to me to marry Tapetheus the daughter of Marres – and she may not become my wife – show it and confirm to me this writing. In the past Tapetheus was the wife of Horion.13

Here the negative formulation survives: if the marriage is not to happen, confirm this ticket. The positive counterpart – if it is to happen – was presumably submitted alongside it. One ticket would be returned. The other would disappear into the temple’s machinery.

The detail about Tapetheus’s previous marriage to Horion is striking. Asklepiades is not merely asking about a marriage in the abstract. He is asking about a specific woman with a known history – a divorcee or a widow. The god is being asked to adjudicate a social situation with real complications. Is she available? Is the union propitious? Is Horion’s shadow still over her? The oracle is being used as what we might call a decision-support system, but one with divine authority backing the output.

sections.title: III. What They Asked
sections.body_html:
The questions people brought to Egyptian oracles cluster around a few persistent anxieties. Travel. Marriage. Legal disputes. Money. Health. The safe return of absent persons.

Neoteros asked about travelling to Alexandria – a journey of several days from the Fayum, involving both expense and the physical risk of Nile transport.14 The legal complaint he hoped to file required appearing before provincial authorities in the great city, navigating a bureaucratic apparatus conducted partly in Latin, partly in Greek, with no guarantee of success. Should he make the investment? The god’s answer would settle the matter.

Asklepiades asked about marriage – a decision with economic, social, and emotional dimensions that a divine verdict could simplify. Other surviving questions from Egyptian oracles follow the same pattern. “Should I remain in Bacchias?” asks one petitioner, facing the universal migrant’s dilemma of whether to stay or move.15 “Am I to be reconciled with my son?” asks another, the question itself a window into family rupture.16

The Sortes Astrampsychi – a late antique oracle book that survives in multiple copies – preserves the full taxonomy of questions that clients were expected to bring. The table of contents reads like a catalogue of human worry:

Will I receive my pay? Will I get the deposit back? Will I buy the property? Will my girlfriend stay with me? Will my first wife stay with me? Am I going to be a magistrate? Can I see my homeland? Have I been poisoned? – help yourself!17

That last entry – “you have been poisoned; help yourself” – is a reminder that oracle answers were not always comforting. The system was designed to include bad outcomes. Some tickets in the lottery delivered precisely the verdict you feared.

sections.title: IV. The Soldiers' Shrine
sections.body_html:
Five hundred kilometres southeast of the Fayum, in the Eastern Desert of Egypt, Roman soldiers consulted a different kind of oracle.

The praesidium of Dios was one of dozens of small fortified way-stations along the road from Coptos on the Nile to the Red Sea port of Berenike – a trade route that funnelled Indian spices, Chinese silk, African ivory, and Arabian incense into the Roman Empire.18 Garrisoned by small detachments of auxiliary soldiers and supplied by camel caravans, these praesidia were among the most isolated postings in the Roman world. Water was rationed. Heat was extreme. Boredom and danger alternated.

Inside the fortlet of Dios, excavated by Helene Cuvigny and her team in the early 2000s, stood a shrine. Dedicated to Zeus Helios Megas Sarapis and the Tyche – the Fortune – of the praesidium, it was a small room with a podium for cult statues, its walls covered in graffiti left by visitors over decades.19 Buried in the fill of the podium, the excavators found a collection of oracle texts inscribed on pottery sherds – ostraca – dating to roughly 175-225 CE.

These texts were pre-written oracle responses, each numbered and associated with a particular deity. They functioned as a system of lot divination: you drew a number, and the corresponding text gave you your fortune.

The surviving responses are terse, poetic, and strangely moving. Number 2, associated with Apollo, reads:

Open the gates that are easy to loose – you have passable paths. Proceed on the road before you, and be known to mortals for deeds, not words.20

Number 4, attributed to Typhon – a distinctly ominous patron – warns:

What you are attempting in your heart, do not do, knowing that it is not expedient. For the road has been established…21

Number 18, marked “for the whole day,” encourages:

You will see what you wish – do not be afraid. Life will take a turn for the better. The ground is firm. It will be for the best.22

Number 21 directly addresses the anxiety of travel – the defining concern of soldiers and merchants on this desolate road:

You will see your companion. Do not fear the waves of the sea. Do not suspect the storm – nothing hard shall befall you. Pray to the gods with confidence, and they themselves will guide you where you go. What you fear, do not heed, and do not be afraid.23

Number 25 uses the language of agricultural hope:

As a farmer, when he sows, hopes for a harvest, so what you desire you shall attain, without envy.24

Number 26 invokes the image of Italian ships – a reminder that these desert soldiers were part of a Mediterranean world:

As a sailor desires to save an Italian ship, so for what you undertake, the road will be fair.25

Number 27 provides the most comprehensive reassurance:

What you ask about, you shall achieve, and it will be completed. You shall grow old in prosperity. Evil you shall never see. The road is fair. The gods will be gracious.26

The system was borrowed from a well-established tradition. These lot oracles closely resemble the astragaloi oracles of southwestern Asia Minor, where dice throws were correlated with deities and verses inscribed on temple walls.27 The soldiers at Dios had imported a Greco-Roman divination technology into the Egyptian desert, adapting it to their needs. The shrine was a franchise, in a sense – a standardised product deployed in a new market.

But there were dark numbers too. Elsewhere in the Eastern Desert, at the fort of Xeron Pelagos – “Dry Sea,” named with the grim humour of soldiers stationed in waterless terrain – oracle tickets were found bearing simple labels: “1. Of Hermes.” “14. Of Ares.” And, ominously: “Of a chthonian daimon.”28 If your lot drew the chthonian spirit of the underworld, the message was clear enough. Some outcomes could not be made gentle.

sections.title: V. What Was at Stake
sections.body_html:
Oracle consultation was not a casual gesture. It was a commitment – an act that bound the petitioner to the answer received. When Neoteros asked whether he should travel to Alexandria, he was not idly wondering. He was submitting his decision to a divine authority whose verdict he intended to follow.

Herodotus, writing six centuries before Neoteros, understood this. His account of Croesus testing the oracles of the Greek world – sending envoys to Delphi, Didyma, Dodona, and other shrines with instructions to ask each oracle what the king of Lydia was doing on the hundredth day – is partly a story about verification and trust.29 Croesus wanted to know which oracles actually worked before committing to one. He was a careful consumer.

But having identified Delphi as reliable, Croesus submitted his genuine question: should he march against the Persians? The oracle replied that if he crossed the Halys River, he would destroy a great empire.30 He crossed. He destroyed an empire – his own. The oracle had been technically correct and practically catastrophic.

The lesson, for the ancient reader, was not that oracles were fraudulent. It was that they were dangerous. Divine communication was real, but human interpretation was fallible. The god spoke truth; the listener heard what he wished to hear. This was the theological framework within which ordinary people like Neoteros and Asklepiades operated. They were not naive. They understood the risk. They consulted anyway, because the alternative – making a consequential decision without divine input – seemed more dangerous still.

sections.title: VI. The Infrastructure of the Invisible
sections.body_html:
The oracle was only one node in a much larger supernatural infrastructure that permeated ancient daily life. This infrastructure was not limited to temples. It lived in private letters, in greetings, in the casual phrases that people used to close their correspondence.

In the third century CE, a man named Didymus wrote to his father Chaeremon from somewhere in Egypt. The letter is entirely mundane: don’t neglect the landowner’s business; mother Thaesis has gone to Antinoopolis for a funeral; write to me about anything you need. Then, in the closing salutations, this:

Give many salutations to my dearest Phullon and his children, whom the evil eye shall not harm, and his wife and those whom we love severally.31

The phrase in Greek is built around the word abaskanta – literally, “unharmed by the evil eye.” It appears as naturally in this letter as “best wishes” appears in a modern one. Didymus is not performing a magical ritual. He is not consulting a specialist. He is simply deploying a standard protective formula, embedded in the grammar of affection, against a threat he considers entirely real.

The evil eye – baskania in Greek, fascinatio in Latin – was perhaps the most pervasive supernatural belief in the ancient Mediterranean.32 It held that certain people could cause harm – illness, death, crop failure, business ruin – simply by looking at something with envy or malice. Children were considered especially vulnerable, because their health and beauty attracted envious attention. The formula abaskanta was deployed specifically for children in letter after letter.

A mother writing to her son Ptolemaeus, who is away studying, closes her letter:

Many salutations are sent to you by your sisters and Theonis’ children, whom the evil eye shall not harm, and by all our friends by name.33

Ammonios, writing to his brother Andronikos about business in Antinoopolis, signs off:

Greet your children, whom I pray may be preserved from the evil eye, and your consort.34

A third-century letter from Cornelius to his mother includes greetings to his sister and to “Herakleas and Mia and their children safe from the evil eye” – ta abaskanta auton tekna.35

This is not magic in the technical sense – no spells, no incantations, no ritual objects. It is something more fundamental: a shared assumption about how the world works, expressed through epistolary convention. The evil eye was as real to these letter-writers as bacteria are to a modern parent wiping down a child’s hands. You could not see the threat, but you knew it was there, and you took basic precautions.

The word abaskantos also functioned as a personal name. A man named Titus Marsyllos Abaskantos appears in a Sicilian inscription of the late third or early fourth century CE – “good and blameless,” his epitaph reads.36 To name a child “Unharmed-by-the-Evil-Eye” was itself an apotropaic gesture, a prayer embedded in an identity. Other families gave children names like Euphemios (“Well-Spoken-Of”) or Kallistos (“Most Beautiful”) and then immediately worried that such names would attract the very attention the evil eye exploited. The paradox was inescapable.

sections.title: VII. The Oracle Book
sections.body_html:
Between the individual questions submitted to temple gods and the lot oracles of desert shrines lay a third technology of divination: the oracle book. These were reference manuals that allowed a client – or a literate intermediary – to generate divine answers through a standardised random procedure.

The Sortes Astrampsychi, the most widely attested of these books, survives in multiple manuscript traditions dating from the third century CE onward.37 Its structure was ingenious. The book contained a numbered list of questions – the standard anxieties catalogued above – and a grid of answers linked to numbers generated by a randomising mechanism (usually dice). You identified your question, performed the random operation, and looked up the corresponding answer.

The answers themselves cover the full range of human outcomes:

You will not buy what you have in mind… You will not get the money just yet… You receive a gratuity… You are restored to your place… You cannot make a contract with another person… Where you are going, you will not stay… You receive your pay… You receive the deposit.38

And then, buried among the financial and romantic predictions:

You have been poisoned. Protect yourself.39

You will not be reconciled with your masters.40

Your escape goes unnoticed.41

A second oracle book, dating to after 231 CE, preserves a remarkable table of “gods who give oracles and signs” – a divine directory listing numbered deities alongside reference numbers pointing to their associated oracle texts. The list reads like a religious census of Roman Egypt: Leto, Sarapis, Ischuys, Agathos Daimon, Ge (Earth), Astraphe (Lightning), Ephialtes, Eros, Thetis, Hekate, Kronos, Apollo, Asklepios, Aphrodite, Ammon, Anubis, Belos, Boubastis, Hera, the Dioscuri, Herakles, Helios, Dionysos, Hephaistos, the Muses, Mithras, Ourania, Osiris, Poseidon, and the Mother of the Gods.42

Greek gods stand beside Egyptian ones. Mithras – the soldiers’ mystery cult – appears alongside Boubastis, the cat-goddess of Bubastis. Eros and Hekate, Ammon and Anubis, the Dioscuri and Asklepios: the oracle book made no theological distinctions. All gods were potential sources of answers. The system was polytheist in the most practical sense – any divine patron might deliver the right number on any given day.

sections.title: VIII. Theodora and Joseph
sections.body_html:
Five centuries after Neoteros asked Soxis Pnepheros about his trip to Alexandria, someone in the Fayum wrote another oracle question. The god had changed. The format had not.

O God of the Christians, is it your will that we should give Theodora, your servant, to Joseph? Yes. Yes.43

The papyrus dates to between 501 and 700 CE. It was found at Krokodilopolis – the ancient “City of Crocodiles,” later renamed Arsinoe – in the Fayum, the same region where Neoteros had consulted his crocodile god.44 The question is about marriage, the same subject that had brought Asklepiades to the shrine of Soknopaios five centuries earlier. The answer – “Yes. Yes” – may have been written by a priest or selected by lot from paired tickets.

The continuity is exact. The structure of the question follows the same pattern as its pagan predecessors: address to the deity, identification of the petitioner or subject, statement of the proposed action, request for divine confirmation. The only difference is the addressee. “To Soxis Pnepheros, greatest god” has become “O God of the Christians.”

This was not an accident, and it was not an isolated survival. AnneMarie Luijendijk’s work on the “Gospel of the Lots of Mary” – a fifth- or sixth-century Coptic miniature codex from the shrine of Saint Kollouthos at Antinoopolis – has demonstrated that Christian lot divination operated on a large scale at Egyptian pilgrimage sites, despite being formally forbidden by church councils and Christian leaders.45

The Gospel of the Lots of Mary contains thirty-seven oracular statements, introduced by the heading: “The Gospel of the lots of Mary, the mother of the Lord Jesus Christ, she to whom Gabriel the archangel brought the good news.” The responses are replete with biblical language but fundamentally practical in orientation:

This matter that you want to do – its time has not yet come.46

The technology is the same as the Sortes Astrampsychi: randomised selection from a pre-written set of answers. The theology has been Christianised – Mary replaces Aphrodite, biblical phrases replace Homeric echoes – but the mechanism and the need are identical.

The church’s position was officially hostile. The Council of Vannes in 461 CE condemned clergy who practised “sortes” – lot divination – from any text, whether biblical or otherwise.47 Augustine argued against the use of scripture for divination, even while acknowledging that the practice was widespread among Christians.48 But prohibition and practice coexisted, as they always do when an institution tries to suppress a technology that satisfies a deep human need.

In Egypt, the coexistence was especially vivid. The shrine of Saint Kollouthos at Antinoopolis – where Luijendijk believes the Gospel of the Lots of Mary was used – was a healing and divination centre that drew pilgrims from across the region. Christians came there to receive answers about their lives, exactly as their ancestors had come to the temples of Soknopaios and Soxis Pnepheros.49 The saints had replaced the crocodile gods. The questions remained.

sections.title: IX. The Sortes in Their World
sections.body_html:
The survival of oracle books across the pagan-Christian divide illuminates something important about the sociology of divination. These were not elite products. The Sortes Astrampsychi, with its questions about pay, deposits, property, girlfriends, and poison, was designed for people in the middle and lower reaches of Roman society – soldiers, tradesmen, small farmers, women wondering about marriage and childbirth.50

The questions themselves constitute a sociological document of extraordinary richness. Consider the full range from the surviving oracle book:

Will I buy the property? Will my girlfriend stay with me? Will my first wife stay with me? Am I getting a release from my girlfriend? Am I going to be a magistrate? Can I see my homeland? Am I completing what I have undertaken? Am I receiving a legacy? Will the one who is abroad come back?51

These are the questions of people who live in a world of economic uncertainty, frequent travel, fragile relationships, and contested inheritances. The absent person – the soldier, the trader, the emigrant – haunts the oracle books as he haunts the private letters. “Will the one who is abroad come back?” is a question that wives, mothers, and creditors all asked, for different reasons.

“Am I going to be a magistrate?” reveals the aspiration toward local office – the dekaprotos or bouleutai positions that conferred status but also crushing financial obligations. “Am I receiving a legacy?” speaks to the anxious waiting that accompanied every wealthy relative’s decline. “Will my first wife stay with me?” and “Am I getting a release from my girlfriend?” map the complicated emotional territory of a society where divorce was relatively easy, concubinage was common, and romantic attachments could overlap with legal marriages in uncomfortable ways.

The oracle book was, in this sense, a mirror of its society. It reflected the full range of anxieties that people carried, and it offered, through the randomising mechanism, a way of resolving the paralysis that accompanies major decisions. The god’s answer – whichever god, whichever answer – provided the external authority that allowed the petitioner to act.

sections.title: X. The Evil Eye as Infrastructure
sections.body_html:
If oracle consultation was the decision-making arm of the everyday supernatural, the evil eye was its security apparatus. It operated not through temples or priests but through the dispersed vigilance of every household.

The evidence from private letters is overwhelming in its consistency. Across centuries, across the pagan-Christian divide, across differences of class and education, the evil eye appears as a taken-for-granted threat requiring routine prophylaxis.

The letter from Cornelius to his mother – a late third- or early fourth-century text from unknown provenance – is typical. The business of the letter concerns fish-sauce spatulas, tow purchases, and a promissory note owed by a linen-worker named Pathermouthis. The supernatural enters only in the closing formula: “I greet my lady sister […] and Herakleas and Mia and their children safe from the evil eye.”52 Seven talents owed by Pathermouthis; a spatula of fish sauce; ten litrai of tow; and the evil eye. These belong to the same texture of life.

The letter from the mother of Ptolemaeus – a second- or third-century text from Oxyrhynchus – is addressed to a boy away at school. The mother has been monitoring his education through intermediaries. She knows he is reading “the sixth book” (probably of Homer). She is concerned about his attendant. The letter is that of an anxious, attentive parent. And then the closing: “Theonis’ children, whom the evil eye shall not harm.”53

A letter from the same archive – P.Oxy. 8.1159, late third century – closes a discussion of corn deposits, tools, expenses, and cushion-retrieving with: “Salute my children, whom the evil eye shall not harm, and our mother and your sister and all our friends.”54

The pattern is remarkably stable. In every case, the evil eye formula appears in the closing greetings, attached specifically to children, deployed as a prophylactic alongside entirely mundane social pleasantries. The letter-writer does not stop to explain the evil eye, does not describe it, does not express doubt about its reality. It is axiomatic. It is infrastructure.

The personal name Abaskantos – “Unharmed-by-the-Evil-Eye” – appears repeatedly in the documentary record, used by both pagans and Christians.55 An ostracon from second-century Egypt lists names for what appears to be an administrative or fiscal purpose: “Pamonthos son of Pachnoumis. Apollos son of Pet[…]. Abaskantos. Sisois and Psenchonsis.”56 The man named Abaskantos walks through this tax register as a living prayer, his identity and his protection fused into a single word.

A German papyrologist named John Noel Adams, analysing the evil eye in Greco-Roman letters, concluded that the formula functioned as what sociolinguists call a “phatic communion” – a standardised expression that maintains social bonds without necessarily conveying informational content, like “How are you?” or “Take care.”57 But this analysis, while structurally accurate, underestimates the degree to which the phrase carried genuine prophylactic intent. These writers were not merely performing a social convention. They were doing something – invoking protection for vulnerable children against a force they believed to be real.

sections.title: XI. The Economics of the Invisible
sections.body_html:
Divination was not free. The temple oracle required a payment – probably modest, but real – to the priests who administered the system. The oracle book required a literate intermediary, or literacy itself, and possibly a set of dice or knucklebones. The lot oracle at a desert shrine required nothing but access, but access to a military fortlet was itself restricted.

The institutional economics of Egyptian temples in the Roman period have been studied in some detail. Temples derived income from land, endowments, and the services they provided – including oracles, healing, dream interpretation, and the sale of amulets.58 The priests of Soknopaios at Soknopaiou Nesos were simultaneously religious officials, landowners, administrators, and tax intermediaries. A declaration from 216 CE by Aurelius Pakysis, “priest and stolistes of the temple of the first rank of the village of Soknopaiou Nesos,” records his property holdings and his role in the temple hierarchy.59

The oracle service was part of this economy. A petitioner who brought a question to the temple also brought a fee, an offering, perhaps a sacrifice. The question itself, written on papyrus, consumed a material resource. The answer, once given, might be kept as an amulet – a physical object with continuing value. The entire cycle was embedded in an economy of the sacred that generated revenue, employed specialists, and consumed materials.

The Sortes oracle books represented a different economic model – a decentralised, potentially democratised form of divination that could operate without temple infrastructure. A literate person with a copy of the book and a set of dice could offer oracular services anywhere. This may help explain why the church was so anxious to suppress the practice: it was uncontrolled, unlicensed, and theologically unsupervised. Anyone could set up shop.

sections.title: XII. The Continuity That Matters
sections.body_html:
In the year 6 CE, Asklepiades asked the crocodile god Soknopaios whether he could marry Tapetheus. In 21-22 CE, Neoteros asked Soxis Pnepheros whether he should travel to Alexandria. Around 175-225 CE, soldiers at Dios pulled lot oracle tickets associated with Apollo, Typhon, and Kronos. Around the same period, soldiers at Xeron Pelagos drew tickets marked “Of Hermes,” “Of Ares,” “Of a chthonian daimon.” In the fifth or sixth century, someone opened the Gospel of the Lots of Mary at the shrine of Saint Kollouthos. Between 501 and 700 CE, someone asked “O God of the Christians” about marrying Theodora to Joseph.

Across these seven centuries, the following things changed: the gods addressed, the language of the formulae, the specific theological justification for the practice, the institutional setting (temple, shrine, monastery, church), and the legal status of divination (permitted, regulated, forbidden, covertly practised).

The following things did not change: the need for divine guidance in decisions involving travel, marriage, money, and risk. The technology of the lot – randomisation plus divine attribution. The written format of the question – address to deity, statement of problem, request for answer. The belief that supernatural forces permeated daily life and required routine management through formulae, names, and protective language.

The evil eye letters span the same centuries without interruption. First-century soldiers, second-century mothers, third-century businessmen, fourth-century Christians – all greeted their correspondents’ children with the same formula, abaskanta, shielding them against the same invisible threat.60

This is not the story of superstition yielding to reason, or of paganism yielding to Christianity in a simple replacement. It is the story of a deep infrastructure – an architecture of the invisible – that persisted because it addressed needs that no political or theological revolution could abolish. People needed to make decisions under uncertainty. People needed to protect their children from harm they could not see. People needed to believe that the universe was responsive to their concerns – that a question, properly framed and properly submitted, would receive an answer.

The papyri preserve this infrastructure with an intimacy that monumental architecture and literary texts cannot match. The Parthenon tells us that Greeks built temples. Plato tells us that Greek philosophers debated the nature of the divine. But a scrap of papyrus from the Fayum, bearing a question addressed to a crocodile god about a trip to Alexandria, tells us how an individual man, on a specific day, in a specific year, navigated his life with the help of the invisible.

Neoteros son of Marreous, year 8 of Tiberius. He asked the god. We do not know what the god said.

notes.text: Corpus ID 36299 (DCLP 958.957673). Oracle question to the god Soxis Pnepheros, dated to 21-22 CE. Found at Karanis in the Fayum. The Greek text reads: Σόξιτι Πνεφερῶτι θεῶι μεγίστωι -- "To Soxis Pnepheros, greatest god."

The dating formula "Year 8 of Tiberius" corresponds to 21-22 CE. Tiberius succeeded Augustus on 19 August 14 CE; his regnal years were counted from that date. Pontius Pilate was appointed prefect of Judaea in approximately 26 CE, though some scholars place his arrival earlier.
Corpus ID 36299. The Greek: εἴ μοι κρίνεται ἐκδημῆσαι εἰς Ἀλεξανδρέαν... καὶ ἔγκλησιν εἰσβάλλω -- "if it is decided for me to travel to Alexandria... and bring a complaint."

On the Fayum as an agricultural landscape sustained by artificial irrigation from the Nile, see D.J. Thompson, "The Infrastructure of Splendour: Census and Tax Records from Roman Egypt," in *Cambridge Ancient History*, vol. 10. The reclamation of Fayum land was a Ptolemaic achievement, expanded under Roman administration.

On the etymology of Pnepheros and its connection to Horus epithets, see Jan Quaegebeur, "Tithoes, Tototoes et les noms de divinites animales," in *Orientalia Lovaniensia Periodica* 13 (1982). The compound form "Soxis Pnepheros" suggests a syncretism between two local manifestations.

On the temples at Karanis, see Elaine K. Gazda, ed., *Karanis, an Egyptian Town in Roman Times: Discoveries of the University of Michigan Expedition to Egypt (1924-1935)* (Ann Arbor: Kelsey Museum of Archaeology, 1983). The two main temples served cults of Pnepheros and Petesouchos, both related to the crocodile god Sobek.

On the dual roles of Egyptian priests as both religious and administrative officials, see Willy Clarysse, "Egyptian Temples and Priests: Graeco-Roman," in *A Companion to Ancient Egypt*, ed. Alan B. Lloyd (Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010).

On the double-ticket oracle procedure, see the Berlin Papyrus Database, "Oracular Question" (berlpap.smb.museum), and Frankfurter, *Religion in Roman Egypt*, pp. 145-152. The positive/negative pair ensured a binary result.

On processional oracle statues in pharaonic Egypt, see Robert K. Ritner, *The Mechanics of Ancient Egyptian Magical Practice* (Chicago: Oriental Institute, 1993). The "nodding" of the statue was a well-attested mechanism for divine communication from the New Kingdom onward.
On the retention of oracle tickets as amulets, see Frankfurter, *Religion in Roman Egypt*, pp. 148-150. The ticket served a double function: answer and protective charm.

The principal collection of Greek oracle questions from the Fayum is discussed in W.M. Brashear, "The Greek Magical Papyri: An Introduction and Survey," in *Aufstieg und Niedergang der romischen Welt* II.18.5 (1995), pp. 3380-3684.

On Soknopaiou Nesos as the primary source of oracle questions, see the Berlin Papyrus Database discussion at berlpap.smb.museum/orakelfrage. The temple of Soknopaios was an oracle and healing shrine of the first rank.

Corpus ID 47329 (AnalPap 16/17.100). Oracle question to Soknopaios, dated 26 April 6 CE. The Greek: εἰ μή μοι κρίνεται γῆμαι Ταπεθεῦν τὴν Μαρρεοῦς θυγατέρα -- "If it is not granted to me to marry Tapetheus the daughter of Marres."

On travel from the Fayum to Alexandria, see Colin Adams, *Land Transport in Roman Egypt* (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2007). The journey typically involved boat transport on the Nile and canal systems.
The question about remaining in Bacchias is discussed in Frankfurter, *Religion in Roman Egypt*, p. 149, drawing on published oracle questions from Fayum temple archives.
Family-related oracle questions are catalogued in H.S. Versnel, "Lot-Oracles," in *Oxford Classical Dictionary*, 4th ed.

Corpus ID 16988 (DCLP, Sortes). The Greek text preserves dozens of standardised oracle answers covering property purchase, money, marriage, travel, poisoning, and other topics. The entry πεφαρμάκωσαι, σεαυτῷ βοήθει -- "You have been poisoned; help yourself" -- appears among routine financial predictions.
On the Eastern Desert trade route, see Steven E. Sidebotham, *Berenike and the Ancient Maritime Spice Route* (Berkeley: University of California Press, 2011).

On the shrine at Dios, see Helene Cuvigny, "The Shrine in the Praesidium of Dios (Eastern Desert of Egypt): Graffiti and Oracles in Context," *Chiron* 40 (2010), pp. 245-299. Reprinted in Cuvigny, ed., *Rome in Egypt's Eastern Desert*, vol. 2 (New York: NYU Press, 2021).
Corpus ID 35509 (DCLP 131.130163). Oracle text no. 2, "Of Apollo": πύλας ἄνοιγε εὐλύτους... πορεύου τὴν προκειμένην ὁδόν, ταχέως δὲ ἔργοις μὴ λόγοις γίνου βροτοῖς.
Corpus ID 35510 (DCLP 131.130164). Oracle text no. 4, "Of Typhon": ὃ ἐπιχείρεις... μὴ πράξῃς εἰδὼς ὅτι οὐ συμφέρει.
Corpus ID 35513 (DCLP 131.130167). Oracle text no. 18, "for the whole day": ὄψῃ ὃ βούλει, μὴ δειλαίνου· ὁ βίος ἕξει ἐπὶ τὸ βέλτιον. ἡ γῆ στερεά.
Corpus ID 35514 (DCLP 131.130168). Oracle text no. 21: μὴ εὐλαβοῦ τὰ κύματα τῆς θαλάσσης. μὴ ὑποπτεύσῃς τὸν χειμῶνα... εὔχου τοῖς θεοῖς πεποιθώς, καὶ αὐτοί σε κυβερνήσουσιν.
Corpus ID 35515 (DCLP 131.130169). Oracle text no. 25: ὥσπερ ἐπιθυμεῖ γεωργὸς ἐὰν σπείρῃ τυχεῖν, οὕτως ὃ ἐπιθυμεῖς καταλάβοις χωρὶς φθόνου.
Corpus ID 35516 (DCLP 131.130170). Oracle text no. 26: ὡς ἐπιθυμεῖ ἰταλικὸν πλοῖον σῶσαι ναύτης, οὕτως εἰς ὃ ἐπιβάλλῃ ἡ ὁδός σοι ἔσται καλή.
Corpus ID 35517 (DCLP 131.130171). Oracle text no. 27: ὑπὲρ ὧν ἐρωτᾷς ἐπιτεύξῃ καὶ τελειωθήσεται. γηράσεις ἐν ἀγαθῷ, κακὸν οὐ μὴ ἴδοις. ἡ ὁδὸς καλή, οἱ θεοὶ εὐίλατοι ἔσονται.

On the astragaloi oracles of Asia Minor, see Fritz Graf, "Rolling the Dice for an Answer," in *Mantike: Studies in Ancient Divination*, ed. S.I. Johnston and P.T. Struck (Leiden: Brill, 2005). The oracles at Dios closely parallel inscribed responses from Kremna and other Anatolian sites.

Corpus IDs 36708, 36709, 36710 (DCLP 370.369038-40). Oracle tickets from Xeron Pelagos: "1. Of Hermes" (α / Ἑρμοῦ), "14. Of Ares" (ιδ / Ἄρεως), and "[...] of a chthonian daimon" (δαίμονος καταχθωνίου). On Xeron Pelagos as a Roman military outpost, see Cuvigny, *Rome in Egypt's Eastern Desert*, vol. 1.

Herodotus, *Histories* 1.46-49. Corpus ID 7577. Croesus tested the oracles by sending envoys on the hundredth day to ask what the king of Lydia was doing at that moment. Only Delphi and the oracle of Amphiaraus gave correct answers.
Herodotus, *Histories* 1.53. Corpus ID 7583. The oracle's response: "If Croesus crosses the Halys, he will destroy a great empire." The ambiguity was famously fatal.
Corpus ID 42659 (P.Oxy. 9.1218). Third-century letter from Didymus to his father Chaeremon. The Greek: τὰ ἀβάσκαντα αὐτοῦ παιδία -- "his children, whom the evil eye shall not harm."

On the evil eye in the ancient Mediterranean, see John H. Elliott, *Beware the Evil Eye: The Evil Eye in the Bible and the Ancient World*, 4 vols. (Eugene, OR: Cascade Books, 2015-2017). The belief is attested across every culture of the ancient Mediterranean, from Mesopotamia to Rome.

Corpus ID 43976 (P.Oxy. 6.930). Second- or third-century letter from a mother to her son Ptolemaeus. She monitors his education through intermediaries and closes with greetings to "Theonis' children, whom the evil eye shall not harm."
Corpus ID 57395 (P.Ryl. 4.604). Third-century letter from Ammonios to Andronikos. The Greek: πρόσειπε τὰ ἀβάσκαντά σου παιδία -- "Greet your children, whom I pray may be preserved from the evil eye."

Corpus ID 26830 (P.Oslo 3.161). Late third- or fourth-century letter from Cornelius to his mother. Closing greetings include "Herakleas and Mia and *ta abaskanta auton tekna* -- their children safe from the evil eye."
Corpus ID 24382 (I.Sicily 001713). Inscription from Sicily, late third to fourth century CE: "Titus Marsyllos Abaskantos, good and blameless" -- *chrestos kai amemptos*.

On the Sortes Astrampsychi, see Randall Stewart, "The Textual Transmission of the Sortes Astrampsychi," *Illinois Classical Studies* 20 (1995), pp. 135-147. Multiple recensions survive, indicating wide circulation.

Corpus ID 16988 (DCLP, Sortes). Sample answers from the preserved oracle text: οὐκ ἀγοράζεις ὃ ἐνθυμεῖσαι (you will not buy what you have in mind); λαμβάνεις τὸ ὀψώνιον (you receive your pay); λαμβάνεις τὴν παραθήκην (you receive the deposit).

Corpus ID 16988. πεφαρμάκωσαι. σεαυτῷ βοήθει -- "You have been poisoned. Help yourself." The entry πεφαρμάκευσαι. ἀσφαλίζου -- "You have been drugged. Take precautions" -- appears separately in the same text.
Corpus ID 16988. οὐ καταλλάσσῃ τοῖς κυρίοις -- "You will not be reconciled with your masters."
Corpus ID 16988. λανθάνει σου ὃ δρασμός -- "Your escape goes unnoticed."

Corpus ID 35926 (DCLP, Sortes, after 231 CE). The table of oracular gods: Θεοὶ χρηματισταὶ καὶ σημάντορες -- "Gods who give oracles and signs." The list includes Leto, Sarapis, Agathos Daimon, Ge, Astraphe, Eros, Thetis, Hekate, Kronos, Apollo, Asklepios, Aphrodite, Ammon, Anubis, Belos, Boubastis, Hera, the Dioscuri, Herakles, Helios, Dionysos, Hephaistos, the Muses, Mithras, Ourania, Osiris, Poseidon, and others.

Corpus ID 3741 (DCLP 37.36291). Christian oracle question from Krokodilopolis, Fayum, 501-700 CE. The Greek: ὁ θεὸς τῶν Χριστιανῶν, ὅτι θελήματός σου ἐστίν ἵνα δώσωμεν Θεοδώραν τὴν δούλην σου τῷ Ἰωσήφ; ναί ναί.

Krokodilopolis (later Arsinoe, modern Medinet el-Fayum) was the ancient nome capital of the Fayum, originally named after the crocodile cult that dominated the region. On the site, see P.M. Fraser, "Krokodilopolis," in *The Oxford Classical Dictionary*, 4th ed.

AnneMarie Luijendijk, *Forbidden Oracles? The Gospel of the Lots of Mary* (Tubingen: Mohr Siebeck, 2014). The Coptic miniature codex was most likely used at the shrine of Saint Kollouthos in Antinoopolis, a Christian pilgrimage site where divination was practised on a large scale.
Luijendijk, *Forbidden Oracles?*, pp. 58-60. Oracle no. 4 from the Gospel of the Lots of Mary.
The Council of Vannes (461 CE), canon 16, forbade clergy from practising *sortes*, whether from scripture or other texts. See Hefele, *History of the Councils of the Church*, vol. 4.
Augustine, *Epistulae* 55.37, discusses the use of scripture for divination (*sortes biblicae*), condemning the practice while acknowledging it was preferable to consulting pagan oracles.

On the shrine of Saint Kollouthos at Antinoopolis as a healing and divination centre, see Luijendijk, *Forbidden Oracles?*, ch. 3; and Dominic Montserrat, "Pilgrimage to the Shrine of SS Cyrus and John at Menouthis in Late Antiquity," in *Pilgrimage and Holy Space in Late Antique Egypt*, ed. D. Frankfurter (Leiden: Brill, 1998).

On the social audience of the Sortes Astrampsychi, see William Hansen, "The Winning of Hippodameia," *Transactions of the American Philological Association* 130 (2000), pp. 19-40, and Randall Stewart, "Sortes Astrampsychi," in *Brill's New Pauly*.
Corpus ID 16988. Selected questions from the Sortes table of contents, reconstructed from the surviving answer categories. Each category of answer presupposes a corresponding standard question.
Corpus ID 26830 (P.Oslo 3.161). See note 35.
Corpus ID 43976 (P.Oxy. 6.930). See note 33.
Corpus ID 22953 (P.Oxy. 8.1159). Late third-century letter closing: "Salute my children, whom the evil eye shall not harm" -- ἀσπάζου μοι τὰ τέκνα μου, ἃ ὁ ὀφθαλμὸς μὴ βάσκῃ.

On Abaskantos as a personal name, see H. Solin and O. Salomies, *Repertorium nominum gentilium et cognominum Latinorum* (Hildesheim: Olms, 1994). The name is attested across the Roman world, particularly in the eastern provinces.
Corpus ID 51833 (O.Bodl. 2.1929). Second-century ostracon listing names: "Pamonthos son of Pachnoumis. Apollos son of Pet[...]. Abaskantos. Sisois and Psenchonsis."

On the phatic function of evil eye formulae in letters, see the analysis in Elliott, *Beware the Evil Eye*, vol. 2, ch. 5 (on Greece and Rome), drawing on Bronislaw Malinowski's concept of "phatic communion."

On temple economics in Roman Egypt, see Clarysse, "Egyptian Temples and Priests" (note 7), and J.G. Manning, *The Last Pharaohs: Egypt Under the Ptolemies, 305-30 BC* (Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2010), ch. 7.
Corpus ID 47354 (BGU 1.321). Declaration by Aurelius Pakysis, priest and stolistes of the temple of Soknopaiou Nesos, dated 7 April 216 CE.

The chronological range of evil eye formulae in Egyptian letters extends from the early Roman period (Corpus ID 29399, BGU 1.249, ca. 75-85 CE) through the fourth century and beyond. The formula remained structurally stable across this entire span.

sources.translation:
To the very great and mighty god Soknopaios, from Asklepiades son of Areios. If it is not granted to me to marry Tapetheus the daughter of 
Marres –  and she may not become my wife – show it and confirm to me this writing. In the past Tapetheus was the wife of Horion. Year 35 of Caesar, 1 Pachon.

Χαιρήμ ονι ... .. ..
sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 9
              Didymus to his father Chaeremon, very many greetings. Do not neglect the things to be done for the land-holder, as you desired, for I know your goodness and reasonableness. There is nothing unpleasant at your house. My mother Thaësis went, I think, to Antinodpolis for a funeral. Tell me freely about anything which you want and I will do it gladly. Give many salutations to my dearest Phullon and his children, whom the evil eye shall not harm, and his wife and those whom we love severally. All your relatives and your children salute you. I pray for your health.

sources.translation: [...] you used to [...] so that also well the [...] I received then from the wife of Attion the comb and the spatula of fish sauce. So do everything, buy for me ten litrai of tow and send them. I greet my lady sister [...] and Herakleas and Mia and their children safe from the evil eye. I greet your brother and your father. I pray for your health for many years, my lady mother. I also sent you a promissory note with this letter, so that you may demand from Pathermouthis the linen-worker the talents specified in the promissory note, seven talents. If you are unable to demand payment from him, ask Aphthonios, and he will collect them for you. If he tries to deceive you, write to me, and I will have him brought here inside. [...] from Cornelius, the son.

sources.translation:
‘… that my honoured leader is in good health. And now therefore I have written about him to brother Eutolmius, and I have written about brother Heraiscus to the same Eutolmius, introducing him to those to whom you are to give the letter. Heraiscus should be present along with you. Attend to your children and your household, as I asked you, and write to me about anything you want in Antinoupolis, for I am pleased with you. Greet your children, whom I pray may be preserved from the evil eye, and your consort. Brother Apunchis salutes you. Forward under seal to Alexandria to brother Latkios the letter enclosed with this. I pray for your health, brother.
‘To Theodosius by a safe friend because it is of great importance to our friends.’
               
            
            
               
                   ‘From Ammonius (xystophorus?) to brother Andronicus.’

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 6
              ... do not hesitate to write to me about anything which you require. It grieved me to learn from the daughter of our teacher Diogenes that he had sailed, for I had no anxiety about him, knowing that he intended to look after you to the best of his ability. I took care to send and ask about your health and learn what you are reading; he said that it was the sixth book and testified at length concerning your attendant. So my son, I urge both you and your attendant to take care that you go to a suitable teacher. Many salutations are sent to you by your sisters and Theonis’ children, whom the evil eye shall not harm, and by all our friends by name. Salute your esteemed attendant Eros ... (Addressed) ... to her son Ptolemaeus.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 8
              ... that I may not trouble him about provisions, since I wrote to him that I was coming by the 30th; otherwise, arrange with him that he shall come by the 13th Phamenoth. With regard to the deposit of corn, do not neglect to worry Thonius. With regard to my tools, tell the men that I am sending the expenses for them, and leave the tools with them until I send the expenses. When you come, bring the old cushion that is up in the dining-room. Salute my children, whom the evil eye shall not harm, and our mother and your sister and all our friends.

sources.translation:
To Aurelios Didymos strategus of the merides of Themistos and Polemon of the Arsinoite from Aurelios Pakysis son of Tesenouphis, 
priest and stolistes of the temple of the first rank of the village of Soknopaiou Nesos of the meris of Herakleides. 
 I possess a place in the house of the wife of my son Aurelios Tesenouphis in the hamlet of Pisaïs in the meris of Themistos in which I keep in storage foodstuffs for our daily use.
 Just lately my relatives went into the place because I myself was away in Alexandria and it was discovered that the foodstuffs had been pilfered. 
 The cause of the theft, as was clear, was that the crime had been committed when the place, which is on the upper floor, had been holed through the floor. 
 When those who are living there were proved to have done this through malice aforethought, they undertook in the presence of both the archephodos of the village and others to give me seven artabas of grain against the amount of the theft. 
 But since they have agreed to the undertaking but up until now have not complied with the restitution, of necessity I am making submission of a petition which I request be placed on record, 
so that my rights be secured for me against the accused, Panouphis son of Stotoetis and Pakysis son of Kanneis. Farewell.  (2nd hand) I, Aureliou (sic) Pakysis, have submitted the petition. 
 (1st hand) Year 24 of Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus Pius Augustus, Pharmouthi 12. 
Translation with minor modifications: J. Whitehorne, BASP 40 (2003), 203-204

sources.translation: And when he sent to test these shrines he gave the Lydians these instructions: they were to keep track of the time from the day they left Sardis, and on the hundredth day inquire of the oracles what Croesus, king of Lydia, son of Alyattes, was doing then; then they were to write down whatever the oracles answered and bring the reports back to him. Now none relate what answer was given by the rest of the oracles. But at Delphi, no sooner had the Lydians entered the hall to inquire of the god and asked the question with which they were entrusted, than the Pythian priestess uttered the following hexameter verses: “I know the number of the grains of sand and the extent of the sea, And understand the mute and hear the voiceless. The smell has come to my senses of a strong-shelled tortoise Boiling in a cauldron together with a lamb's flesh, Under which is bronze and over which is bronze.”

sources.translation: The Lydians who were to bring these gifts to the temples were instructed by Croesus to inquire of the oracles whether he was to send an army against the Persians and whether he was to add an army of allies. When the Lydians came to the places where they were sent, they presented the offerings, and inquired of the oracles, in these words: “ Croesus, king of Lydia and other nations, believing that here are the only true places of divination among men, endows you with such gifts as your wisdom deserves. And now he asks you whether he is to send an army against the Persians, and whether he is to add an army of allies.” Such was their inquiry; and the judgment given to Croesus by each of the two oracles was the same: namely, that if he should send an army against the Persians he would destroy a great empire. And they advised him to discover the mightiest of the Greeks and make them his friends.
